questions.statement: Monopolistically competitive firms can make abnormal profits in the long run.
questions.payload: Low barriers to entry attract new firms, eroding abnormal profits until only normal profits remain.

questions.statement: In perfect competition, individual firms are price takers.
questions.payload: Correct. No single firm is large enough to influence the market price — they must accept it.

questions.statement: A monopolist always produces at the point where price equals marginal cost.
questions.payload: A monopolist maximises profit where MR = MC, and since price exceeds MR, price is above marginal cost.

questions.statement: In perfect competition, firms earn abnormal profits in the long run.
questions.payload: Free entry of new firms competes away abnormal profits until all firms earn normal profit only.

questions.statement: An oligopoly is characterised by a large number of small firms.
questions.payload: An oligopoly has a small number of large, dominant firms — high market concentration is its defining feature.

questions.statement: A monopolist faces a downward-sloping demand curve.
questions.payload: Correct. As the sole supplier, the monopolist faces the entire market demand — which slopes downward.

questions.statement: Product differentiation is a key feature of perfect competition.
questions.payload: Perfect competition requires homogeneous products. Product differentiation is a feature of monopolistic competition.

questions.statement: Under monopolistic competition, there are high barriers to entry.
questions.payload: Monopolistic competition has low barriers to entry — it's similar to perfect competition in this respect.

questions.statement: Allocative efficiency occurs when price equals marginal cost.
questions.payload: Correct. At P = MC, every unit produced is worth exactly what it costs to make — resources are optimally allocated.

questions.statement: A natural monopoly arises when one firm can supply the entire market at a lower cost than multiple competing firms.
questions.payload: Correct. High fixed costs mean a single firm benefits from economies of scale that smaller competitors can't match.

questions.statement: In an oligopoly, firms are mutually interdependent in their decision-making.
questions.payload: Correct. Each firm must consider rivals' likely reactions before changing price or output.

questions.statement: A cartel is a formal agreement among firms in a competitive market to compete aggressively.
questions.payload: A cartel is a collusive agreement to restrict output and raise prices — the opposite of competing aggressively.

questions.statement: Productive efficiency is achieved when a firm produces at the lowest point of its average total cost curve.
questions.payload: Correct. At minimum ATC, the firm is producing at the lowest possible cost per unit.

questions.statement: Monopolies always lead to deadweight loss.
questions.payload: Correct. By restricting output and raising price above MC, monopolies create a welfare loss — output that would benefit society isn't produced.

questions.statement: In perfect competition, the firm's demand curve is the same as its marginal revenue curve.
questions.payload: Correct. Because price is fixed (horizontal demand), every additional unit sold adds exactly the market price to revenue.

questions.statement: Non-price competition is common in oligopolies.
questions.payload: Correct. Oligopolists often avoid price wars — competing instead through advertising, branding, and product quality.

questions.statement: A firm in monopolistic competition produces at allocative efficiency in the long run.
questions.payload: Monopolistically competitive firms price above MC in the long run — producing at allocative inefficiency.
